channel.title: Player Choice on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Player Choice on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Restricting Content Based On Player Choice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about restricting content based on player choice.

What do I mean by that? I’m talking about if you’ve ever played a game where there’s some content that you cannot see, based on something that you chose to do with your player. I’m not talking about restrictions on an options menu. I’m not talking about like you decided to play the lowest difficulty mode and because of that, you won’t see a certain hard boss monster. Or, when you do, they don’t use certain attacks with certain special effects, because that doesn’t occur unless you’re playing it on normal or hard difficulty.

item.title: Telegraphing Consequences
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about telegraphing consequences.

This is something I’ve talked about a ton on this channel, yet I don’t have a video about it I can’t point anybody to any one place and go: “Oh, that’s where I talk about it”. In fact, uh, viewer Soon Hull asked: “You mentioned telegraphing long-term playability. How is that done And how are other? What are other things to telegraph for the player. And have you done that in the past?

item.title: Compromising For The Medium
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about compromising for the medium and basically

this is a talk about how you can’t have everything you want in your video game, because it’s a video game. The nature of video gaming prevent or should very much discourage you from trying to do certain things, and I want to talk about that because it falls under the bigger categories. You can’t have everything you want, now I’m not talking about the compromises that you may have to introduce because you’re working in a team and other people have different ideas and different things they want to try. I have a whole working in teams video I’ll link below. I’m not talking about compromise because you or the team members don’t have the skills needed to implement the design ideas that you’ve created. I have a whole video about matching projects to teams.

item.title: Botched Quests And Player Freedom
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about botched quests and player Freedom.

This comes from a question from Nick Berg, one who asked: can you share your ideas on botched, failed quests? Don’t these limit the freedom of making your own story and RPGs? I actually think it expands your freedom. So I think that’s a good question and I want to explain my answer. I think Nick is referring to a video I did on Quest implementation and I will link it below.

item.title: NPC Reactions
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC reaction.

This comes from a question from AliceLittle1865, who asked: can you make a video, maybe on the topic of NPCs and companions? What I mean is how to create character reactions when they interact with the world or not, for example, how all the companions in New Vegas have some kind of reaction when on a mission or part of the map or player choice or faction. The same could be applied to a tabletop RPG. When the DM creates a character that the players have in their party or not, how would that character react to the interactions and development of the story and how do you prepare for that? I want to break this into two parts because the, at least for a video game, the complicated thing is how to set it up so you can do the reaction, and then I’ll talk very briefly on what things you might want to react to. Also, I call this NPC reaction. It is: commonly used for companion reaction, since companions are with you, are following you around and they see a lot of what you do. But I called this NPC reaction because you can really use it for anything in any character in the game who’s not the player. We’ve all played games where the NPCs react to things you do and say: my games all do that. To me that’s one of the just wonderful beautiful things about an RPG is reacting to what the player does and a big chunk of that reaction comes from having non-player characters say: I saw what you did, I saw what you said.

item.title: Believable Reactivity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about believable reactivity and, in other words, how do you make NPCs react to player choices in a way that makes sense in the RPG that you’re making?

This was a good question from Chaser 7, 2886, who asked: I was wondering how do you make choices in RPGs believable? What I mean is how do NPCs know you made the choice you made without making it like NPC’s magically know you made whatever choice you made? Okay, first of all, this is a good question, and I know I say that a lot, but this is a good question for two different reasons. One, thank you, Chaser 7, for actually clarifying your question. When you just said how do you make choices in RPGs believable, I thought you were going to say how do you make the NPC asking the quest to give to the player, how do you make that sound like a believable ask, like would the king actually ask me to go find his missing?

item.title: Story Telling And Player Agency
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about storytelling and player agency.

This is something I’ve touched on in a ton of videos. I will try to link as many as I can below, but there are a lot of them. I wanted to make a specific video on this because I kind of feel like the need to laser focus on the fact that storytelling and player agency are a little contradictory. They’re a little bit at odds and at some point if you want to make a computer role playing game, you need to decide, like consciously, which one of those takes precedence in your game. Now, of course, I have a bias here. I love player agency over all else.

item.title: Beyond Fighting, Sneaking, and Talking
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to brainstorm ideas with you on ways RPGs can move past the fighting, sneaking, talking Triad that, let’s face it, has been done to death since the 90s.

I mean, yeah, that’s kind of what we pushed really hard with. Fallout was: can you fight past things, can you sneak past things, can you talk past things? But Fallout was made in 1997.

item.title: Personality Roleplay
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about personality roleplay.

This is the answer to a question from a channel member. Interestingly, they asked the question and I wanted to think about it because I wanted to make sure I answered it correctly, and also since I don’t write dialogues much anymore, I wanted to make sure I’d framed my answer correctly. Anyway, vasenkov asked: lately I’ve been thinking a lot, like a lot, about something I call personality roleplay. Perhaps designers have their own term for it. I’m talking about conversations with multiple reply options that ultimately or immediately lead to the same outcome and are basically just different flavors of the same reply.

item.title: Canonical Endings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about canonical endings, and by canonical endings I mean the ending of the of a game that is considered to be the right ending, or the correct ending, or the true ending or, at the very minimum, the ending the developer expected you to have.

Now, for nonlinear role playing games, that there’s only one ending. Everybody has the exact same experience and then experiences the one ending that there is, and that’s the canonical ending. But for a lot of my games, which were nonlinear, multiple ending, very choice and consequence-based games, finding the canonical story ending can be tough.

item.title: Morality Systems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about morality systems in RPGs.

This is actually a question from the discording tales 8198, who asks: what is your point of view on alignment or Karma systems in RPGs, systems of morality, of moralities, maybe, IE going Beyond good or evil, but not relativistic per se, using different sets of values measured from their own viewpoints? Hm, that is a bit of a vague question, um, but it’s also a difficult one, because now we’re talking, philosophy and definition of Good and Evil, and let me talk about what I tried in a couple games and then what I’m thinking now. So back in Fallout, we tried to do reputation and we just did kind of an overall reputation. So it wasn’t necessarily good or evil, it was. This was a positive thing to do towards your rep and this was a negative thing, and it was done via script and it turned out to be really varied, because what one group of people in on the team thought was a good thing for you to do for your reputation, other people thought was a bad thing. And in the game itself there was: hey, if you do this would be positive only for this one group. It’s a negative for this other group. So I don’t think Fallout was a good first step. It was a, mish mash of ideas. I mean, we had reputation titles in there too, like a champion of some group or you know, the child killer was a reputation title. So when we did Arcanum, I wanted to systematize these things.

item.title: Grey Morality
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gray morality.

This has come up a lot in my games. It’s the situation where you feel like you’ve been presented with a number of options to finish a quest and there’s really no right choice, and sometimes there’s no wrong choice either. Both of them just lead to different things happening, none of which affect you or a lot of other people, and sometimes we, you It’s hard to even describe whether there’s a right or wrong choice, because it’s hard to Define what’s right or wrong.

item.title: How To Gate Non-Linear Stories
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to gate nonlinear stories.

This comes from a question from Galaxy 5 production who asks: when you’re programming a game, how do you make sure things happen in sequence according to a story, not only so that a, b and c happen before D, but also maybe events andf can happen at any time between events A and Z, or maybe, based on player decisions, events change, Etc. Interesting question I do have a video already on non-linear stories, but I didn’t really talk about the gating stage or gating events, which does happen. Um, I’ll give examples from Fallout and Arcanum as I go along. The just kind of explain those ideas and how it kind of changed, how I did things over time. But I want to make something clear and it’s probably just the wording you chose: but this kind of gating happens at your design stage.

item.title: Implementing Endgame Slides
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk to you about implementing endgame slides.

I know I’ve talked about slides before and in a way this is going to be a companion video to the one I did last month on how to do the beginning of video games. But end slides is something that I’ve done in a lot of my games, starting with Fallout. My games are kind of known for having choice and content quence called CNC, which — just a side note — CNC used to refer to the game command and Conquer and for a brief period in the 90s, the CNC Music Factory. That song I got the power very popular in that Simpsons episode where Homer thinks Bart’s gay.

item.title: Player Agency
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim, and let’s start off the new year by talking about player agency.

Now, what do I mean by player agency? Is that when a game agency? Player agency is that when a game allows the player to choose what to do and then the game reacts, especially the storyline of the game. And player agency the opposite of this would be when a game has a predefined, very narrow set of things the player can do, even though it feels like he should be able to do other things, and the storyline is predictable and unchangeable. You’re basically as the player, you’re just. You just move along that story, you’re just there for the ride.

item.title: Social Conflict Resolution
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about social resolutions in RPGs, and by that I mean combat resolutions that are social, not combat conflict resolutions that are social, it’s hard to talk about.

I’m going to answer a question from stavos Nicola, who’s answer, who’s asked several questions so far in this channel. He keeps asking good questions, so I’m going to keep answering them. And he asked asks: how do you feel about social, peaceful resolutions and RPGs? Many RPGs tend to use social persuasion based resolutions for Quest or challenges as a one roll skip combat Choice — rarely games and playy mini games or other forms of social combat that make these social encounters more interesting or even on par with the physical combat resolutions. Do you feel RPGs today sufficiently support the social pill pillar with engaging gameplay and, if not, how would you have liked it to be handled? So I think it’s pretty obvious that my answer is going to be that I like peaceful, social resolutions to conflict um. They’ve been part of every RPG I’ve made for the last 25 years.

item.title: Pacifism In Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about pacifism in games.

This comes from a question from am Brandt 7422, who asks: I wonder what you think about pacifist play Styles and how much implementing one costs on development time. So again, T DW. I love PL pacifist playthroughs and they shouldn’t take much extra work, if any at all, to support.

Uh, but before I dive into this, though, two quick things. Uh, YouTube changed the algorithm again, so I have almost no control over ads anymore. You may have heard about this, but I can’t control if they’re at the beginning or the end or if they’re skippable. The only thing I can control is whether or not I want them to be in the middle of the video, and I’ve turned that off, and that is the one control I still have, sorry.

item.title: Player Hoarding
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about player hoarding.

Mr awesome 5211 asks: what suggestions would you have regarding player hoarding when it comes to items and skills throughout the game? That’s a good question and I know, I know you everyone’s had the experience where they end a game and they have a thousand heal potions or that one Scroll of Doom that you were waiting for just the right moment to use, or us. You had a heel entire party spell that you’re you never cast because you never came across a point where everyone in the party was hurt.

item.title: Non-Linear Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about nonlinear game design.

This is something I’ve been doing ever since Fallout, so these ideas date back to 1994, but I see more and more games doing things like this these days, and I love it because this is my favorite kind of game. Now, what I mean by a nonlinear game design is how do you make a world and the instances within it that are nonlinear, meaning the player can choose the order they want to visit them in. Now, for me and my games, this comes with the extra difficulty mode of I always want to let the player make any kind of a character they wanted. I really don’t like RPGs that force you to play a particular character. Um, I my attitude is you make the story, I make the character? If you’re going to make both, then maybe I’ll just go watch a movie, but I want your story, though, to react to my character, and that’s where the fun comes in.

item.title: The Origin of Reactivity
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the origin of reactivity in my computer games.

I had a lot of fun talking about the origin of dumb characters and the original flawed characters, and all this goes back to early tabletop games that I would play with friends and co-workers. Now I already talked a little bit about reactivity when I talked about how, when I was teaching groups at interplay to people who never played, I made a single dungeon and ran three groups through it and they all — it all — was wildly different and people thought the dungeon was different. Now it was a tiny six room dungeon.
